contraventionDescription: Failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicles – Contravention Code 52

sections.heading: Failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicles – Contravention Code 52
sections.content:
Local authorities are now enforcing this contravention using CCTV enforcement. Quite often however the signs are not sufficiently clear.
Suffixes:
52g) goods vehicles exceeding max gross weight indicated
52m) motor vehicles
52s) solo motorcycles
52v) all vehicles except non-mechanically propelled ones being pushed
52x) motor vehicles except solo motorcycles
sections.contentHtml:
Local authorities are now enforcing this contravention using CCTV enforcement. Quite often however the signs are not sufficiently clear.

Suffixes:
52g) goods vehicles exceeding max gross weight indicated
52m) motor vehicles
52s) solo motorcycles
52v) all vehicles except non-mechanically propelled ones being pushed
52x) motor vehicles except solo motorcycles

sections.heading: Sample signs:
sections.content:
52g) Goods vehicles exceeding the gross weight indicated on the goods vehicle symbol prohibited.
52s) Solo motorcycles prohibited.
52m) No Motor Vehicles
52v) No Vehicles
52x) Motor vehicles except solo motorcycles prohibited.
sections.contentHtml:
52g) Goods vehicles exceeding the gross weight indicated on the goods vehicle symbol prohibited.

 

52s) Solo motorcycles prohibited.

 

52m) No Motor Vehicles

 

52v) No Vehicles

 

52x) Motor vehicles except solo motorcycles prohibited.

sections.heading: How to have your penalty charge notice (PCN) for this parking contravention cancelled.
sections.content: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.
sections.contentHtml: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.

sections.heading: Follow our 3 stage process:
sections.content:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
2 Check: What a Penalty Charge Notice must contain
3 Check: Statutory grounds for appeal for Moving Traffic Contraventions
sections.contentHtml:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)

2 Check: What a Penalty Charge Notice must contain

3 Check: Statutory grounds for appeal for Moving Traffic Contraventions

sections.heading: Specific grounds of appeal for this contravention
sections.content:
Was the PCN sufficiently clear as to the contravention. In one case the PCN stated failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicle, however the signage at the location stated motor vehicles prohibited. The adjudicator said the PCN should have referred to “motor vehicles” rather than “certain types of vehicle”. Certain types of vehicles is too general. The contraventions have various suffixes. The correct suffix must be used and the contravention stated must be the exact one that relates to the stated contravention code.
In July 2012 an adjudicator found against Camden council on the basis that the signage was inadequately clear at the notorious Grafton Road Junction where the council had issued over £5m of penalty charge notices. If you have received a PCN at this location see the adjudicators decisions.
There are 11 more ways that you can have your penalty charge notice cancelled for this offence. We have even summarised adjudicator’s decisions where motorists have won.
Go to top
 
As the contravention is that of “failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicles,” it is important to remember that in order to prove this contravention that the enforcing authority must actually show you passing the sign and continuing to drive into and along the street where certain vehicles are prohibited. It is not sufficient to simply show your vehicle. You can ask to see the video or photographic evidence. This only applies if the contravention states “failing to comply with a sign” However regardless there must be clear signage as otherwise the contravention is unenforceable.
Was the weight of your vehicle within the limit. If so then you should send a copy of the vehicle registration document to the local authority.
Was the signage clear, and placed before the restrictions actually start to allow you sufficient time to take an alternative route?
Were there any other restrictions in force? For example at one location we have seen 2 signs on the same pole. One for this contravention and one for contravention 33c (route for use by buses and pedal cycles only) To have 2 restrictions is confusing and this may well be a basis for an appeal.
We have evidence of some councils continuing to enforce restrictions such as these even though adjudicators have ruled that their signage is unclear.
Were there road markings, if so did they contain exactly the same restrictions as the signs. Appeals have been upheld where wording on the road did not exactly match the sign.
Were you loading/unloading? Sometimes there will be an exemption relating to loading to premises within the zone. If you were loading within the street, or in fact drove through the street but parked elsewhere in order to return to the street to make a delivery, then you may not have committed a contravention.
Were you displaying a disabled badge as quite often there are concessions relating to disabled badge holders.
If there are restrictions at certain times were times stated on the Penalty charge notice (PCN) actually the same as on the signs.
The PCN must also show a picture of the sign which is essential if the contravention is “failing to comply with a sign” etc
Did you commit the contravention because you had no alternative. In one case a lorry blocked the road so the motorist had no alterative. The appeal was allowed.
Were you carrying out a delivery as most TMO’S (traffic management orders will allow deliveries)
Also were there signs warning of CCTV camera enforcement in the vicinity. Whilst these are not compulsory under the traffic management act they are compulsory under the guidelines issued by the information commissioner’s office (ICO)There is no guarantee however that an adjudicator would rule in your favour as we had a case where he did not. although in another case the adjudicator did!
Remember that you may cite compassionate grounds when appealing against a Penalty Charge Notice
Now take a look at the adjudicators decisions for this contravention.
 
Go to top
 
Adjudicators decisions for failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicles
CASE No 2120306288 – London Borough Of Camden – Grafton Road July 2012 –
Camden council have issued over £5m of penalty charge notices at this location and now finally an adjudicator has confirmed what we all knew “that the signage was inadequately clear” Anthony Chan agreed with the appellant that the white plate under the no motor vehicles sign showing the times of the restriction is not authorised under TSRGD 2002 as the only permitted sign is one that shows the exception to the prohibition ie the hours that the restriction does not operate rather than the hours that it does operate.which the current sign shows.
 
CASE NO: 206 0543587 – London Borough of Camden
Grafton Road, NW5
In this case, the Appellant had received two Penalty Charge Notices but did not actually understand what he had done wrong.  The Council provided evidence that there were in fact “motor vehicles prohibited” signs, one either side of the carriageway although the PCN referred to the contravention of failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicle (rather than “motor vehicle”).  The Penalty Charge Notice should have referred to motor vehicles as opposed to certain types of vehicle.  The Penalty Charge Notice also did not include a picture of the sign which is essential as the offence is failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicle.  The adjudicator stated in the circumstances, I find that the PCN fails to comply with the requirements of Section 4(8)(a)(i) of the 2003 London Local Authorities and Transport for London Act which provides that a Penalty Charge Notice must state the grounds on which the Council or, as the case may be Transport for London, believe that the Penalty Charge Notice is payable with respect to the vehicle.  I find that the PCN is insufficiently clear and fails to inform the motorist that the prohibition applies to motor vehicles.
The adjudicator also referred to his colleague’s case which also found against Camden at the same location and on similar grounds.
 
CASE NO: 206 0500165 – London Borough of Harrow
Camrose Avenue
This case was upheld because the signs at the location which were perfectly clear and proper indicated that only buses and cycles were allowed to use the route.  “However, they have combined these with a road marking indicating BUSES ONLY.  This appears to be a variant of diagram 1048.3 but not a permitted variant.  Even if it were a permitted variant it might only be conjoined with diagram 953 and its permitted variant without the cycle symbol, not as here where the cycle symbol was included”.  The Appeal was upheld.   Basically, what the adjudicator was saying is that the signage allowed buses and cycles but the road markings allowed buses only.  There are very strict regulations as to the signage and road markings that must be in place and if they are not correct then the prohibition is unenforceable as indeed it was in this case.
 
CASE NO: 206 0449341 – London Borough of Islington
Market Road, London N7
The contravention in this case was “that the Appellant failed to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on a particular vehicle (weighing over more than 7.5tons).  To prove this contravention, the Authority must first establish the sign in question”.  “I have studied the CCTV evidence and this does not show the sign in question nor do the stills in the Penalty Charge Notice”.  He went on to say that “the Local Authority has not proved this contravention and therefore the Appellant must be allowed.  Finally, he went on to say “but where on the face of it, the vehicle looks as if it exceeds the permitted weight it is for the Appellant if he denies that it does to rebut this presumption by producing a copy of the DVLA registration document that will specify the weight”.  As with most moving traffic contraventions, the contravention is actually failing to comply with a sign indicating….and therefore if it is not proven that correct signage was in situ at the location, the offence is unenforceable and furthermore pay be illegal for the authority to continue to enforce it.
 
CASE NO: 206 0170140 – London Borough of Newham
Park Avenue, Junction of Flanders Road
This is an amusing case because in fact it was the London Borough of Southwark who was the Appellant.  One of their vehicles had been issued a Penalty Charge Notice by the Borough of Newham.  The Appeal was upheld and we can do no better than quote the adjudicator’s only paragraph: “The alleged contravention is failing to comply with a traffic sign.   There was no evidence of the traffic sign before me.  I am not satisfied that the alleged contravention occurred”.  As with most moving traffic contraventions, the contravention is actually failing to comply with a sign indicating….and therefore if it is not proven that correct signage was in situ at the location, the offence is unenforceable and furthermore pay be illegal for the authority to continue to enforce it.
 
CASE NO: 206 0552668 – London Borough of Lambeth
Strathleven Road
We can do no better than quote the paragraph from the adjudicator: “The Appellant tells me and I accept that the post with the sign on it was facing in the wrong direction.  Certainly the signs served by the local authority are unclear and equivocal and there is no CCTV footage provided.  The Appeal is allowed”.  Not only must there be clear signage on both sides of the road but the signs must be facing in the right direction!
 
CASE NO: 206 0487588 – London Borough of Camden
Grafton Road
In this case, the Appellant stated that she had no alternative at the time due to a lorry blocking the road but to drive along the prohibited route.  The Local Authority, Camden, did not agree.  However, the adjudicator stated “I accepted that she had, at the time, no alternative but to drive away from the garage in the direction which took her through the prohibition sign. Consequently the Appeal is allowed”.  It would seem that such a restriction is unenforceable where motorists have no alternative route to take due to road works or the blocking of the road.
 
CASE NO: 207 0072385 – London Borough of Newham
Park Avenue, Flanders Road
A most amusing case where the enforcement camera was stolen.  The local authority sought to rely on an officer’s statement which referred to the recording equipment but as there were no photographs or video of the car the Appeal was allowed!  Why would the Council try and pursue a Penalty Charge Notice when they had no photographic evidence that the contravention actually took place.
 
CASE NO: 206 0557399 – London Borough of Islington
Riversdale Road, London N5
The adjudicator could not identify, from the video and the stills produced by the local authority, the sign and as “neither the video film or the stills showed the vehicle contravening any sub-signage” the Appeal was allowed.  There was also no evidence that the weight of the vehicle had exceeded 7.5tons.  The Appeal was allowed.  Once again, the contravention is failing to comply with a sign and it is incumbent on the Local Authority to produce evidence that the signs were clear, correctly installed, facing the right direction and not obscured.
 
CASE NO: 207 0015337 – London Borough of Lambeth 
Strathleven Road
The Appeal was allowed because the Appellant stated that the vehicle photographed was not actually his vehicle, ie it had been cloned.  The adjudicator said there was a difference in the style and the format of the number plates and the Appellant’s monologue is missing from the number plate which has been photographed.  The CCTV footage which I have watched a number of times was of very poor quality”.  The Appeal was allowed.  We hear more and more about vehicles being cloned.  Obviously you are not liable to pay a Penalty Charge Notice if it did not relate to your vehicle.
 
CASE NO: 206 0565535 – London Borough of Camden
Lambs Conduit Street, WC1
The vehicle was making a delivery to a property in Lambs Conduit Street although the video evidence showed the vehicle driving along the prohibited route and out the other side.  However, the Traffic Management Order stated that goods could be loaded or unloaded at “any premises in Lambs Conduit Street”.  The Appeal was allowed because although the premises to which goods were being delivered was outside the prohibited section of Lambs Conduit Street, the Traffic Management Order referred to the whole of Lambs Conduit Street. The Appeal was allowed.  It is always worth checking the Traffic Management Order as quite often vehicles are prohibited in only one part of the street but if the Traffic Management Order allows loading in the whole street, then you are entitled to drive through the prohibited section.
 
CASE NO: 207 0151324 – Hammersmith & Fulham
Fulham Road
The Appellant passed the sign which prohibited all motor vehicles.  “But argues that no breach occurred because he dismounted in Fulham Road and pushed the machine only remounting and riding off into Moorpark Road once passed the sign”.  The Appellant stated “the signs referred to only a prohibition of motor vehicles at the actual location of the sign and not before it or after it.  The fact that I did this is not, I believe, an offence”.
The adjudicator stated that “I do not accept that pushing rather than riding a motorcycle passed the sign prohibiting motor vehicles makes any difference to whether a contravention has occurred”.    The Appeal would not have been allowed on this basis.  However, the prohibited area was only a few meters in length and the sign prohibiting motor vehicles were erected at the end of the stretch rather than the beginning so that at the time a motorist would have reached the sign, they would already have committed a contravention so the Appeal was allowed on this basis.  An interesting point which was used on another similar case at this exact location where a motorist dismounted and wheeled his motorcycle across the small prohibited area.  As the motorcycle would still have been caused to pass the signs, whether or not it had been ridden or not, a contravention would have occurred.  Luckily in this case, the Council have positioned the signs at the end of the restricted area rather than the beginning!
 
CASE NO: 207 0152950 – London Borough of Harrow
Camrose Avenue
This Appeal was allowed because “the local authority has failed to provide any evidence of the signage with which it is alleged there was failure to comply.  I have, on previous occasions, allowed Appeals on the basis of that signage of restrictions on Camrose Avenue was not compliant with the Traffic Signs Regulations and General Directions 2002”.  The Appeal was allowed.  It is an absolute disgrace that a local authority would continue to enforce restrictions when they know their signage does not comply.  This is also illegal.
 
CASE NO: 206 0096740 – Camden Council
Laystall Street
This was an interesting one as the Council rejected the Applicant’s formal Appeal but on the Notice of Rejection it referred to the Applicant’s Right of Appeal “28 days from the date hereof” whereas the Act requires the reference to say “28 days from the date of service” – which must, ‘inevitably’ be after 28 days from the date of issue.   This is the second Appeal to be upheld on this basis, the first was by the National Parking Adjudication Service in the case of Lukha v Aylesbury Vale District Council.  The Appeal was allowed.  Not only must the wording on the Penalty Charge Notice comply but also the text on the Notice of Rejection must comply.
 
CASE NO: 207 0107195 – Borough of Islington
Morris Place
This is an interesting case as the Appellant said he did not receive the initial Penalty Charge Notice because it may either have been intercepted or because there were so many flats in his block and the postman delivered to a central point.  The adjudicator said “that the crucial Penalty Charge Notice was not served effectively that is to say ‘actually delivered’”.   The Appeal was allowed.  The local authority has to prove that the Penalty Charge Notice is correctly served.  Simply posting it to a motorist is not proof that the motorist actually received it – an interesting point!
 
CASE NO: 206 0292035 – Transport for London
Battersea Bridge
The position on this is that the owner of the vehicle said that he did not receive the original Penalty Charge Notice.   Transport for London could only show that the PCN was printed.  But, in any event, the adjudicator accepted that the Appellant did not receive the Penalty Charge Notice and therefore the Appeal was allowed.   It is up to the local authority to ensure that a Penalty Charge Notice is received.
 
CASE NO: 207 0117279 – Transport for London
Battersea Bridge Road/ Westbridge Road
This is an interesting case where a vehicle was hired but that the person who received the Penalty Charge Notice said that it was not his signature on the hire agreement.  The adjudicator said that “the position in law is that if the hire agreement is not signed, the Appellant is not deemed to be the owner.  On the evidence as it stands, I am not satisfied that the signature on the hiring agreement is the Appellant’s and I am therefore not satisfied that he is liable as the owner of the vehicle”.  The Appeal was allowed.  If you have not hired a vehicle or signed the hire agreement, then liability for a Penalty Charge Notice cannot be passed to you.
 
CASE NO: 207 000963A – London Borough of Croydon
North End Croydon
The adjudicator was satisfied that the contravention occurred but that the owner had sold his car and produced the letter from the DVLA confirming this.   The Appeal was allowed.  You are not liable for a Penalty Charge Notice in respect of a vehicle that you did not own at the time.
 
206 – 0543532 – London Borough of Islington
Leonard Street
This case was won because the Notice of Rejection issued by the Local Authority was not compliant with Schedule 6, paragraph 4 of the Road Traffic Act 1991 which states as follows:-
“Where any representations are made under paragraph 2 above but the Local Authority concerned do not accept that a ground has been established, the Notice served under paragraph 2(7) above (the Notice of Rejection) must:

a)    state that a Charge Certificate may be served under Paragraph 6 below unless before the end of the period of 28 days beginning with the date of service of the Notice of Rejection:

(i)    the Penalty Charge Notice is paid; or
(ii)    the person on whom the Notice is served appeals to a parking adjudicator against a Penalty Charge;

b)    Indicate the nature of a parking adjudicator’s power to award costs against any person appealing to him; and
c)    Describe in general terms the form and manner in which an Appeal to a parking adjudicator must be made and may contain such other information as the Authority consider appropriate.

However, the Notice of Rejection issued by Islington in this instance stated: Therefore within 28 days of the date of this letter, you should either pay the Penalty Charge or Appeal against our decision and if you do not do either – pay or appeal within the 28 day period, we may issue a Charge Certificate.”
It was argued that the wording does not make it clear that the period commences from the date of service of the letter and not the date of the letter itself and that therefore the Notice of Rejection is not compliant.  The Appellant supported his case by reference to another case at MPAS which was Case No: AY05003B (which was Lukha v Aylesbury District Council).
The adjudicator found in favour of the Appellant and stated “I am satisfied that the above authorities indicate that a strict interpretation is required when considering the mandatory requirements of the statute.  The Notice of Rejection in this case does not comply with those requirements and is therefore invalid and accordingly I must allow the Appeal”.  There are very strict guidelines on not just the wording of a Penalty Charge Notice but also other Notices that may be served during the process such as, in this case, the Notice of Rejection.  It is important to note that the 28 day period section on a Notice of Rejection must state as follows “The enforcing authority may disregard any such representations which are received by them after the period of 28 days beginning with the date on which the Penalty Charge Notice in question was served”.
 
CASE NO: 206 0542755 – City of Westminster
Oxford Street
In this case, the Penalty Charge Notice showed 6th November 2006 and the date of issue of 6th November 2006, however, the audit trail produced by Westminster clearly showed the adjudicator that the Penalty Charge Notice could not have been issued for at least two days after the 6th November.  The adjudicator allowed the Appeal and said “it is not open to the City of Westminster to produce false copies in evidence!”.
 
CASE NO: 206 0164512 – London Borough of Islington
Theberton Street
In this case there was no date of issue on the Notice, only the date of contravention.  Therefore the Appeal was allowed.  A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful even if the 2 dates are the same.
 
CASE NO: 206 0176963 – London Borough of Islington
Theberton Street
As was the case before, the Penalty Charge Notice did not state the date of the Notice “it is therefore invalid and unenforceable and I must accordingly allow this Appeal”. A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful even if the dates are the same.
 
CASE NO: 206 0109514 – City of Westminster
Oxford Street
This is another case where Westminster’s Penalty Charge Notice that did not show the date of the Notice.  The adjudicator said “the PCN issued to the Appellant’s vehicle records the date of the alleged contravention but does not include the “date of the Notice”.  The PCN does not therefore comply with Section 66.  It is consequently invalid and the Appellant is not obliged to pay the Penalty Charge”.  The Appeal was allowed.  A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful.

 
CASE NO: 207 0143971 – Waltham Forest Council
Hoe Street E17
This case concerned a Penalty Charge Notice that was dated over 28 days after the alleged contravention.  Also, no unit of currency was specified for the full penalty charge.  The adjudicator said that “either A or B would be fatal”.  Therefore, the adjudicator did not need to adjudicate.
Quite unbelievable that an authority would try and obtain money in this way.
Back to Moving Traffic
sections.contentHtml:
Was the PCN sufficiently clear as to the contravention. In one case the PCN stated failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicle, however the signage at the location stated motor vehicles prohibited. The adjudicator said the PCN should have referred to “motor vehicles” rather than “certain types of vehicle”. Certain types of vehicles is too general. The contraventions have various suffixes. The correct suffix must be used and the contravention stated must be the exact one that relates to the stated contravention code.

In July 2012 an adjudicator found against Camden council on the basis that the signage was inadequately clear at the notorious Grafton Road Junction where the council had issued over £5m of penalty charge notices. If you have received a PCN at this location see the adjudicators decisions.

There are 11 more ways that you can have your penalty charge notice cancelled for this offence. We have even summarised adjudicator’s decisions where motorists have won.

Go to top

 

As the contravention is that of “failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicles,” it is important to remember that in order to prove this contravention that the enforcing authority must actually show you passing the sign and continuing to drive into and along the street where certain vehicles are prohibited. It is not sufficient to simply show your vehicle. You can ask to see the video or photographic evidence. This only applies if the contravention states “failing to comply with a sign” However regardless there must be clear signage as otherwise the contravention is unenforceable.

Was the weight of your vehicle within the limit. If so then you should send a copy of the vehicle registration document to the local authority.

Was the signage clear, and placed before the restrictions actually start to allow you sufficient time to take an alternative route?

Were there any other restrictions in force? For example at one location we have seen 2 signs on the same pole. One for this contravention and one for contravention 33c (route for use by buses and pedal cycles only) To have 2 restrictions is confusing and this may well be a basis for an appeal.

We have evidence of some councils continuing to enforce restrictions such as these even though adjudicators have ruled that their signage is unclear.

Were there road markings, if so did they contain exactly the same restrictions as the signs. Appeals have been upheld where wording on the road did not exactly match the sign.

Were you loading/unloading? Sometimes there will be an exemption relating to loading to premises within the zone. If you were loading within the street, or in fact drove through the street but parked elsewhere in order to return to the street to make a delivery, then you may not have committed a contravention.

Were you displaying a disabled badge as quite often there are concessions relating to disabled badge holders.

If there are restrictions at certain times were times stated on the Penalty charge notice (PCN) actually the same as on the signs.

The PCN must also show a picture of the sign which is essential if the contravention is “failing to comply with a sign” etc

Did you commit the contravention because you had no alternative. In one case a lorry blocked the road so the motorist had no alterative. The appeal was allowed.

Were you carrying out a delivery as most TMO’S (traffic management orders will allow deliveries)

Also were there signs warning of CCTV camera enforcement in the vicinity. Whilst these are not compulsory under the traffic management act they are compulsory under the guidelines issued by the information commissioner’s office (ICO)There is no guarantee however that an adjudicator would rule in your favour as we had a case where he did not. although in another case the adjudicator did!

Remember that you may cite compassionate grounds when appealing against a Penalty Charge Notice

Now take a look at the adjudicators decisions for this contravention.

 

Go to top

 

Adjudicators decisions for failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicles

CASE No 2120306288 – London Borough Of Camden – Grafton Road July 2012 –

Camden council have issued over £5m of penalty charge notices at this location and now finally an adjudicator has confirmed what we all knew “that the signage was inadequately clear” Anthony Chan agreed with the appellant that the white plate under the no motor vehicles sign showing the times of the restriction is not authorised under TSRGD 2002 as the only permitted sign is one that shows the exception to the prohibition ie the hours that the restriction does not operate rather than the hours that it does operate.which the current sign shows.

 

CASE NO: 206 0543587 – London Borough of Camden
Grafton Road, NW5

In this case, the Appellant had received two Penalty Charge Notices but did not actually understand what he had done wrong.  The Council provided evidence that there were in fact “motor vehicles prohibited” signs, one either side of the carriageway although the PCN referred to the contravention of failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicle (rather than “motor vehicle”).  The Penalty Charge Notice should have referred to motor vehicles as opposed to certain types of vehicle.  The Penalty Charge Notice also did not include a picture of the sign which is essential as the offence is failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicle.  The adjudicator stated in the circumstances, I find that the PCN fails to comply with the requirements of Section 4(8)(a)(i) of the 2003 London Local Authorities and Transport for London Act which provides that a Penalty Charge Notice must state the grounds on which the Council or, as the case may be Transport for London, believe that the Penalty Charge Notice is payable with respect to the vehicle.  I find that the PCN is insufficiently clear and fails to inform the motorist that the prohibition applies to motor vehicles.

The adjudicator also referred to his colleague’s case which also found against Camden at the same location and on similar grounds.

 

CASE NO: 206 0500165 – London Borough of Harrow
Camrose Avenue

This case was upheld because the signs at the location which were perfectly clear and proper indicated that only buses and cycles were allowed to use the route.  “However, they have combined these with a road marking indicating BUSES ONLY.  This appears to be a variant of diagram 1048.3 but not a permitted variant.  Even if it were a permitted variant it might only be conjoined with diagram 953 and its permitted variant without the cycle symbol, not as here where the cycle symbol was included”.  The Appeal was upheld.   Basically, what the adjudicator was saying is that the signage allowed buses and cycles but the road markings allowed buses only.  There are very strict regulations as to the signage and road markings that must be in place and if they are not correct then the prohibition is unenforceable as indeed it was in this case.

 

CASE NO: 206 0449341 – London Borough of Islington
Market Road, London N7

The contravention in this case was “that the Appellant failed to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on a particular vehicle (weighing over more than 7.5tons).  To prove this contravention, the Authority must first establish the sign in question”.  “I have studied the CCTV evidence and this does not show the sign in question nor do the stills in the Penalty Charge Notice”.  He went on to say that “the Local Authority has not proved this contravention and therefore the Appellant must be allowed.  Finally, he went on to say “but where on the face of it, the vehicle looks as if it exceeds the permitted weight it is for the Appellant if he denies that it does to rebut this presumption by producing a copy of the DVLA registration document that will specify the weight”.  As with most moving traffic contraventions, the contravention is actually failing to comply with a sign indicating….and therefore if it is not proven that correct signage was in situ at the location, the offence is unenforceable and furthermore pay be illegal for the authority to continue to enforce it.

 

CASE NO: 206 0170140 – London Borough of Newham
Park Avenue, Junction of Flanders Road

This is an amusing case because in fact it was the London Borough of Southwark who was the Appellant.  One of their vehicles had been issued a Penalty Charge Notice by the Borough of Newham.  The Appeal was upheld and we can do no better than quote the adjudicator’s only paragraph: “The alleged contravention is failing to comply with a traffic sign.   There was no evidence of the traffic sign before me.  I am not satisfied that the alleged contravention occurred”.  As with most moving traffic contraventions, the contravention is actually failing to comply with a sign indicating….and therefore if it is not proven that correct signage was in situ at the location, the offence is unenforceable and furthermore pay be illegal for the authority to continue to enforce it.

 

CASE NO: 206 0552668 – London Borough of Lambeth
Strathleven Road

We can do no better than quote the paragraph from the adjudicator: “The Appellant tells me and I accept that the post with the sign on it was facing in the wrong direction.  Certainly the signs served by the local authority are unclear and equivocal and there is no CCTV footage provided.  The Appeal is allowed”.  Not only must there be clear signage on both sides of the road but the signs must be facing in the right direction!

 

CASE NO: 206 0487588 – London Borough of Camden
Grafton Road

In this case, the Appellant stated that she had no alternative at the time due to a lorry blocking the road but to drive along the prohibited route.  The Local Authority, Camden, did not agree.  However, the adjudicator stated “I accepted that she had, at the time, no alternative but to drive away from the garage in the direction which took her through the prohibition sign. Consequently the Appeal is allowed”.  It would seem that such a restriction is unenforceable where motorists have no alternative route to take due to road works or the blocking of the road.

 

CASE NO: 207 0072385 – London Borough of Newham
Park Avenue, Flanders Road

A most amusing case where the enforcement camera was stolen.  The local authority sought to rely on an officer’s statement which referred to the recording equipment but as there were no photographs or video of the car the Appeal was allowed!  Why would the Council try and pursue a Penalty Charge Notice when they had no photographic evidence that the contravention actually took place.

 

CASE NO: 206 0557399 – London Borough of Islington
Riversdale Road, London N5

The adjudicator could not identify, from the video and the stills produced by the local authority, the sign and as “neither the video film or the stills showed the vehicle contravening any sub-signage” the Appeal was allowed.  There was also no evidence that the weight of the vehicle had exceeded 7.5tons.  The Appeal was allowed.  Once again, the contravention is failing to comply with a sign and it is incumbent on the Local Authority to produce evidence that the signs were clear, correctly installed, facing the right direction and not obscured.

 

CASE NO: 207 0015337 – London Borough of Lambeth
Strathleven Road

The Appeal was allowed because the Appellant stated that the vehicle photographed was not actually his vehicle, ie it had been cloned.  The adjudicator said there was a difference in the style and the format of the number plates and the Appellant’s monologue is missing from the number plate which has been photographed.  The CCTV footage which I have watched a number of times was of very poor quality”.  The Appeal was allowed.  We hear more and more about vehicles being cloned.  Obviously you are not liable to pay a Penalty Charge Notice if it did not relate to your vehicle.

 

CASE NO: 206 0565535 – London Borough of Camden
Lambs Conduit Street, WC1

The vehicle was making a delivery to a property in Lambs Conduit Street although the video evidence showed the vehicle driving along the prohibited route and out the other side.  However, the Traffic Management Order stated that goods could be loaded or unloaded at “any premises in Lambs Conduit Street”.  The Appeal was allowed because although the premises to which goods were being delivered was outside the prohibited section of Lambs Conduit Street, the Traffic Management Order referred to the whole of Lambs Conduit Street. The Appeal was allowed.  It is always worth checking the Traffic Management Order as quite often vehicles are prohibited in only one part of the street but if the Traffic Management Order allows loading in the whole street, then you are entitled to drive through the prohibited section.

 

CASE NO: 207 0151324 – Hammersmith & Fulham
Fulham Road

The Appellant passed the sign which prohibited all motor vehicles.  “But argues that no breach occurred because he dismounted in Fulham Road and pushed the machine only remounting and riding off into Moorpark Road once passed the sign”.  The Appellant stated “the signs referred to only a prohibition of motor vehicles at the actual location of the sign and not before it or after it.  The fact that I did this is not, I believe, an offence”.

The adjudicator stated that “I do not accept that pushing rather than riding a motorcycle passed the sign prohibiting motor vehicles makes any difference to whether a contravention has occurred”.    The Appeal would not have been allowed on this basis.  However, the prohibited area was only a few meters in length and the sign prohibiting motor vehicles were erected at the end of the stretch rather than the beginning so that at the time a motorist would have reached the sign, they would already have committed a contravention so the Appeal was allowed on this basis.  An interesting point which was used on another similar case at this exact location where a motorist dismounted and wheeled his motorcycle across the small prohibited area.  As the motorcycle would still have been caused to pass the signs, whether or not it had been ridden or not, a contravention would have occurred.  Luckily in this case, the Council have positioned the signs at the end of the restricted area rather than the beginning!

 

CASE NO: 207 0152950 – London Borough of Harrow
Camrose Avenue

This Appeal was allowed because “the local authority has failed to provide any evidence of the signage with which it is alleged there was failure to comply.  I have, on previous occasions, allowed Appeals on the basis of that signage of restrictions on Camrose Avenue was not compliant with the Traffic Signs Regulations and General Directions 2002”.  The Appeal was allowed.  It is an absolute disgrace that a local authority would continue to enforce restrictions when they know their signage does not comply.  This is also illegal.

 

CASE NO: 206 0096740 – Camden Council
Laystall Street

This was an interesting one as the Council rejected the Applicant’s formal Appeal but on the Notice of Rejection it referred to the Applicant’s Right of Appeal “28 days from the date hereof” whereas the Act requires the reference to say “28 days from the date of service” – which must, ‘inevitably’ be after 28 days from the date of issue.   This is the second Appeal to be upheld on this basis, the first was by the National Parking Adjudication Service in the case of Lukha v Aylesbury Vale District Council.  The Appeal was allowed.  Not only must the wording on the Penalty Charge Notice comply but also the text on the Notice of Rejection must comply.

 

CASE NO: 207 0107195 – Borough of Islington
Morris Place

This is an interesting case as the Appellant said he did not receive the initial Penalty Charge Notice because it may either have been intercepted or because there were so many flats in his block and the postman delivered to a central point.  The adjudicator said “that the crucial Penalty Charge Notice was not served effectively that is to say ‘actually delivered’”.   The Appeal was allowed.  The local authority has to prove that the Penalty Charge Notice is correctly served.  Simply posting it to a motorist is not proof that the motorist actually received it – an interesting point!

 

CASE NO: 206 0292035 – Transport for London
Battersea Bridge

The position on this is that the owner of the vehicle said that he did not receive the original Penalty Charge Notice.   Transport for London could only show that the PCN was printed.  But, in any event, the adjudicator accepted that the Appellant did not receive the Penalty Charge Notice and therefore the Appeal was allowed.   It is up to the local authority to ensure that a Penalty Charge Notice is received.

 

CASE NO: 207 0117279 – Transport for London
Battersea Bridge Road/ Westbridge Road

This is an interesting case where a vehicle was hired but that the person who received the Penalty Charge Notice said that it was not his signature on the hire agreement.  The adjudicator said that “the position in law is that if the hire agreement is not signed, the Appellant is not deemed to be the owner.  On the evidence as it stands, I am not satisfied that the signature on the hiring agreement is the Appellant’s and I am therefore not satisfied that he is liable as the owner of the vehicle”.  The Appeal was allowed.  If you have not hired a vehicle or signed the hire agreement, then liability for a Penalty Charge Notice cannot be passed to you.

 

CASE NO: 207 000963A – London Borough of Croydon
North End Croydon

The adjudicator was satisfied that the contravention occurred but that the owner had sold his car and produced the letter from the DVLA confirming this.   The Appeal was allowed.  You are not liable for a Penalty Charge Notice in respect of a vehicle that you did not own at the time.

 

206 – 0543532 – London Borough of Islington
Leonard Street

This case was won because the Notice of Rejection issued by the Local Authority was not compliant with Schedule 6, paragraph 4 of the Road Traffic Act 1991 which states as follows:-

“Where any representations are made under paragraph 2 above but the Local Authority concerned do not accept that a ground has been established, the Notice served under paragraph 2(7) above (the Notice of Rejection) must:

  1. a)    state that a Charge Certificate may be served under Paragraph 6 below unless before the end of the period of 28 days beginning with the date of service of the Notice of Rejection:

(i)    the Penalty Charge Notice is paid; or
(ii)    the person on whom the Notice is served appeals to a parking adjudicator against a Penalty Charge;

  1. b)    Indicate the nature of a parking adjudicator’s power to award costs against any person appealing to him; and
  2. c)    Describe in general terms the form and manner in which an Appeal to a parking adjudicator must be made and may contain such other information as the Authority consider appropriate.

However, the Notice of Rejection issued by Islington in this instance stated: Therefore within 28 days of the date of this letter, you should either pay the Penalty Charge or Appeal against our decision and if you do not do either – pay or appeal within the 28 day period, we may issue a Charge Certificate.”

It was argued that the wording does not make it clear that the period commences from the date of service of the letter and not the date of the letter itself and that therefore the Notice of Rejection is not compliant.  The Appellant supported his case by reference to another case at MPAS which was Case No: AY05003B (which was Lukha v Aylesbury District Council).

The adjudicator found in favour of the Appellant and stated “I am satisfied that the above authorities indicate that a strict interpretation is required when considering the mandatory requirements of the statute.  The Notice of Rejection in this case does not comply with those requirements and is therefore invalid and accordingly I must allow the Appeal”.  There are very strict guidelines on not just the wording of a Penalty Charge Notice but also other Notices that may be served during the process such as, in this case, the Notice of Rejection.  It is important to note that the 28 day period section on a Notice of Rejection must state as follows “The enforcing authority may disregard any such representations which are received by them after the period of 28 days beginning with the date on which the Penalty Charge Notice in question was served”.

 

CASE NO: 206 0542755 – City of Westminster
Oxford Street

In this case, the Penalty Charge Notice showed 6th November 2006 and the date of issue of 6th November 2006, however, the audit trail produced by Westminster clearly showed the adjudicator that the Penalty Charge Notice could not have been issued for at least two days after the 6th November.  The adjudicator allowed the Appeal and said “it is not open to the City of Westminster to produce false copies in evidence!”.

 

CASE NO: 206 0164512 – London Borough of Islington
Theberton Street

In this case there was no date of issue on the Notice, only the date of contravention.  Therefore the Appeal was allowed.  A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful even if the 2 dates are the same.

 

CASE NO: 206 0176963 – London Borough of Islington
Theberton Street

As was the case before, the Penalty Charge Notice did not state the date of the Notice “it is therefore invalid and unenforceable and I must accordingly allow this Appeal”. A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful even if the dates are the same.

 

CASE NO: 206 0109514 – City of Westminster
Oxford Street

This is another case where Westminster’s Penalty Charge Notice that did not show the date of the Notice.  The adjudicator said “the PCN issued to the Appellant’s vehicle records the date of the alleged contravention but does not include the “date of the Notice”.  The PCN does not therefore comply with Section 66.  It is consequently invalid and the Appellant is not obliged to pay the Penalty Charge”.  The Appeal was allowed.  A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful.

 

CASE NO: 207 0143971 – Waltham Forest Council
Hoe Street E17

This case concerned a Penalty Charge Notice that was dated over 28 days after the alleged contravention.  Also, no unit of currency was specified for the full penalty charge.  The adjudicator said that “either A or B would be fatal”.  Therefore, the adjudicator did not need to adjudicate.

Quite unbelievable that an authority would try and obtain money in this way.

Back to Moving Traffic

sections.heading: Adjudicators decisions for failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicles
sections.content:
CASE No 2120306288 – London Borough Of Camden – Grafton Road July 2012 –
Camden council have issued over £5m of penalty charge notices at this location and now finally an adjudicator has confirmed what we all knew “that the signage was inadequately clear” Anthony Chan agreed with the appellant that the white plate under the no motor vehicles sign showing the times of the restriction is not authorised under TSRGD 2002 as the only permitted sign is one that shows the exception to the prohibition ie the hours that the restriction does not operate rather than the hours that it does operate.which the current sign shows.
CASE NO: 206 0543587 – London Borough of Camden
Grafton Road, NW5
In this case, the Appellant had received two Penalty Charge Notices but did not actually understand what he had done wrong.  The Council provided evidence that there were in fact “motor vehicles prohibited” signs, one either side of the carriageway although the PCN referred to the contravention of failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicle (rather than “motor vehicle”).  The Penalty Charge Notice should have referred to motor vehicles as opposed to certain types of vehicle.  The Penalty Charge Notice also did not include a picture of the sign which is essential as the offence is failing to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on certain types of vehicle.  The adjudicator stated in the circumstances, I find that the PCN fails to comply with the requirements of Section 4(8)(a)(i) of the 2003 London Local Authorities and Transport for London Act which provides that a Penalty Charge Notice must state the grounds on which the Council or, as the case may be Transport for London, believe that the Penalty Charge Notice is payable with respect to the vehicle.  I find that the PCN is insufficiently clear and fails to inform the motorist that the prohibition applies to motor vehicles.
The adjudicator also referred to his colleague’s case which also found against Camden at the same location and on similar grounds.
CASE NO: 206 0500165 – London Borough of Harrow
Camrose Avenue
This case was upheld because the signs at the location which were perfectly clear and proper indicated that only buses and cycles were allowed to use the route.  “However, they have combined these with a road marking indicating BUSES ONLY.  This appears to be a variant of diagram 1048.3 but not a permitted variant.  Even if it were a permitted variant it might only be conjoined with diagram 953 and its permitted variant without the cycle symbol, not as here where the cycle symbol was included”.  The Appeal was upheld.   Basically, what the adjudicator was saying is that the signage allowed buses and cycles but the road markings allowed buses only.  There are very strict regulations as to the signage and road markings that must be in place and if they are not correct then the prohibition is unenforceable as indeed it was in this case.
CASE NO: 206 0449341 – London Borough of Islington
Market Road, London N7
The contravention in this case was “that the Appellant failed to comply with a sign indicating a prohibition on a particular vehicle (weighing over more than 7.5tons).  To prove this contravention, the Authority must first establish the sign in question”.  “I have studied the CCTV evidence and this does not show the sign in question nor do the stills in the Penalty Charge Notice”.  He went on to say that “the Local Authority has not proved this contravention and therefore the Appellant must be allowed.  Finally, he went on to say “but where on the face of it, the vehicle looks as if it exceeds the permitted weight it is for the Appellant if he denies that it does to rebut this presumption by producing a copy of the DVLA registration document that will specify the weight”.  As with most moving traffic contraventions, the contravention is actually failing to comply with a sign indicating….and therefore if it is not proven that correct signage was in situ at the location, the offence is unenforceable and furthermore pay be illegal for the authority to continue to enforce it.
CASE NO: 206 0170140 – London Borough of Newham
Park Avenue, Junction of Flanders Road
This is an amusing case because in fact it was the London Borough of Southwark who was the Appellant.  One of their vehicles had been issued a Penalty Charge Notice by the Borough of Newham.  The Appeal was upheld and we can do no better than quote the adjudicator’s only paragraph: “The alleged contravention is failing to comply with a traffic sign.   There was no evidence of the traffic sign before me.  I am not satisfied that the alleged contravention occurred”.  As with most moving traffic contraventions, the contravention is actually failing to comply with a sign indicating….and therefore if it is not proven that correct signage was in situ at the location, the offence is unenforceable and furthermore pay be illegal for the authority to continue to enforce it.
CASE NO: 206 0552668 – London Borough of Lambeth
Strathleven Road
We can do no better than quote the paragraph from the adjudicator: “The Appellant tells me and I accept that the post with the sign on it was facing in the wrong direction.  Certainly the signs served by the local authority are unclear and equivocal and there is no CCTV footage provided.  The Appeal is allowed”.  Not only must there be clear signage on both sides of the road but the signs must be facing in the right direction!
CASE NO: 206 0487588 – London Borough of Camden
Grafton Road
In this case, the Appellant stated that she had no alternative at the time due to a lorry blocking the road but to drive along the prohibited route.  The Local Authority, Camden, did not agree.  However, the adjudicator stated “I accepted that she had, at the time, no alternative but to drive away from the garage in the direction which took her through the prohibition sign. Consequently the Appeal is allowed”.  It would seem that such a restriction is unenforceable where motorists have no alternative route to take due to road works or the blocking of the road.
CASE NO: 207 0072385 – London Borough of Newham
Park Avenue, Flanders Road
A most amusing case where the enforcement camera was stolen.  The local authority sought to rely on an officer’s statement which referred to the recording equipment but as there were no photographs or video of the car the Appeal was allowed!  Why would the Council try and pursue a Penalty Charge Notice when they had no photographic evidence that the contravention actually took place.
CASE NO: 206 0557399 – London Borough of Islington
Riversdale Road, London N5
The adjudicator could not identify, from the video and the stills produced by the local authority, the sign and as “neither the video film or the stills showed the vehicle contravening any sub-signage” the Appeal was allowed.  There was also no evidence that the weight of the vehicle had exceeded 7.5tons.  The Appeal was allowed.  Once again, the contravention is failing to comply with a sign and it is incumbent on the Local Authority to produce evidence that the signs were clear, correctly installed, facing the right direction and not obscured.
CASE NO: 207 0015337 – London Borough of Lambeth 
Strathleven Road
The Appeal was allowed because the Appellant stated that the vehicle photographed was not actually his vehicle, ie it had been cloned.  The adjudicator said there was a difference in the style and the format of the number plates and the Appellant’s monologue is missing from the number plate which has been photographed.  The CCTV footage which I have watched a number of times was of very poor quality”.  The Appeal was allowed.  We hear more and more about vehicles being cloned.  Obviously you are not liable to pay a Penalty Charge Notice if it did not relate to your vehicle.
CASE NO: 206 0565535 – London Borough of Camden
Lambs Conduit Street, WC1
The vehicle was making a delivery to a property in Lambs Conduit Street although the video evidence showed the vehicle driving along the prohibited route and out the other side.  However, the Traffic Management Order stated that goods could be loaded or unloaded at “any premises in Lambs Conduit Street”.  The Appeal was allowed because although the premises to which goods were being delivered was outside the prohibited section of Lambs Conduit Street, the Traffic Management Order referred to the whole of Lambs Conduit Street. The Appeal was allowed.  It is always worth checking the Traffic Management Order as quite often vehicles are prohibited in only one part of the street but if the Traffic Management Order allows loading in the whole street, then you are entitled to drive through the prohibited section.
CASE NO: 207 0151324 – Hammersmith & Fulham
Fulham Road
The Appellant passed the sign which prohibited all motor vehicles.  “But argues that no breach occurred because he dismounted in Fulham Road and pushed the machine only remounting and riding off into Moorpark Road once passed the sign”.  The Appellant stated “the signs referred to only a prohibition of motor vehicles at the actual location of the sign and not before it or after it.  The fact that I did this is not, I believe, an offence”.
The adjudicator stated that “I do not accept that pushing rather than riding a motorcycle passed the sign prohibiting motor vehicles makes any difference to whether a contravention has occurred”.    The Appeal would not have been allowed on this basis.  However, the prohibited area was only a few meters in length and the sign prohibiting motor vehicles were erected at the end of the stretch rather than the beginning so that at the time a motorist would have reached the sign, they would already have committed a contravention so the Appeal was allowed on this basis.  An interesting point which was used on another similar case at this exact location where a motorist dismounted and wheeled his motorcycle across the small prohibited area.  As the motorcycle would still have been caused to pass the signs, whether or not it had been ridden or not, a contravention would have occurred.  Luckily in this case, the Council have positioned the signs at the end of the restricted area rather than the beginning!
CASE NO: 207 0152950 – London Borough of Harrow
Camrose Avenue
This Appeal was allowed because “the local authority has failed to provide any evidence of the signage with which it is alleged there was failure to comply.  I have, on previous occasions, allowed Appeals on the basis of that signage of restrictions on Camrose Avenue was not compliant with the Traffic Signs Regulations and General Directions 2002”.  The Appeal was allowed.  It is an absolute disgrace that a local authority would continue to enforce restrictions when they know their signage does not comply.  This is also illegal.
CASE NO: 206 0096740 – Camden Council
Laystall Street
This was an interesting one as the Council rejected the Applicant’s formal Appeal but on the Notice of Rejection it referred to the Applicant’s Right of Appeal “28 days from the date hereof” whereas the Act requires the reference to say “28 days from the date of service” – which must, ‘inevitably’ be after 28 days from the date of issue.   This is the second Appeal to be upheld on this basis, the first was by the National Parking Adjudication Service in the case of Lukha v Aylesbury Vale District Council.  The Appeal was allowed.  Not only must the wording on the Penalty Charge Notice comply but also the text on the Notice of Rejection must comply.
CASE NO: 207 0107195 – Borough of Islington
Morris Place
This is an interesting case as the Appellant said he did not receive the initial Penalty Charge Notice because it may either have been intercepted or because there were so many flats in his block and the postman delivered to a central point.  The adjudicator said “that the crucial Penalty Charge Notice was not served effectively that is to say ‘actually delivered’”.   The Appeal was allowed.  The local authority has to prove that the Penalty Charge Notice is correctly served.  Simply posting it to a motorist is not proof that the motorist actually received it – an interesting point!
CASE NO: 206 0292035 – Transport for London
Battersea Bridge
The position on this is that the owner of the vehicle said that he did not receive the original Penalty Charge Notice.   Transport for London could only show that the PCN was printed.  But, in any event, the adjudicator accepted that the Appellant did not receive the Penalty Charge Notice and therefore the Appeal was allowed.   It is up to the local authority to ensure that a Penalty Charge Notice is received.
CASE NO: 207 0117279 – Transport for London
Battersea Bridge Road/ Westbridge Road
This is an interesting case where a vehicle was hired but that the person who received the Penalty Charge Notice said that it was not his signature on the hire agreement.  The adjudicator said that “the position in law is that if the hire agreement is not signed, the Appellant is not deemed to be the owner.  On the evidence as it stands, I am not satisfied that the signature on the hiring agreement is the Appellant’s and I am therefore not satisfied that he is liable as the owner of the vehicle”.  The Appeal was allowed.  If you have not hired a vehicle or signed the hire agreement, then liability for a Penalty Charge Notice cannot be passed to you.
CASE NO: 207 000963A – London Borough of Croydon
North End Croydon
The adjudicator was satisfied that the contravention occurred but that the owner had sold his car and produced the letter from the DVLA confirming this.   The Appeal was allowed.  You are not liable for a Penalty Charge Notice in respect of a vehicle that you did not own at the time.
206 – 0543532 – London Borough of Islington
Leonard Street
This case was won because the Notice of Rejection issued by the Local Authority was not compliant with Schedule 6, paragraph 4 of the Road Traffic Act 1991 which states as follows:-
“Where any representations are made under paragraph 2 above but the Local Authority concerned do not accept that a ground has been established, the Notice served under paragraph 2(7) above (the Notice of Rejection) must:
a)    state that a Charge Certificate may be served under Paragraph 6 below unless before the end of the period of 28 days beginning with the date of service of the Notice of Rejection:
(i)    the Penalty Charge Notice is paid; or
(ii)    the person on whom the Notice is served appeals to a parking adjudicator against a Penalty Charge;
b)    Indicate the nature of a parking adjudicator’s power to award costs against any person appealing to him; and
c)    Describe in general terms the form and manner in which an Appeal to a parking adjudicator must be made and may contain such other information as the Authority consider appropriate.
However, the Notice of Rejection issued by Islington in this instance stated: Therefore within 28 days of the date of this letter, you should either pay the Penalty Charge or Appeal against our decision and if you do not do either – pay or appeal within the 28 day period, we may issue a Charge Certificate.”

Oxford Street

Quite unbelievable that an authority would try and obtain money in this way.

CASE NO: 206 0487588 – London Borough of Camden
Grafton Road

In this case, the Appellant stated that she had no alternative at the time due to a lorry blocking the road but to drive along the prohibited route.  The Local Authority, Camden, did not agree.  However, the adjudicator stated “I accepted that she had, at the time, no alternative but to drive away from the garage in the direction which took her through the prohibition sign. Consequently the Appeal is allowed”.  It would seem that such a restriction is unenforceable where motorists have no alternative route to take due to road works or the blocking of the road.

 

CASE NO: 207 0072385 – London Borough of Newham
Park Avenue, Flanders Road

A most amusing case where the enforcement camera was stolen.  The local authority sought to rely on an officer’s statement which referred to the recording equipment but as there were no photographs or video of the car the Appeal was allowed!  Why would the Council try and pursue a Penalty Charge Notice when they had no photographic evidence that the contravention actually took place.

 

CASE NO: 206 0557399 – London Borough of Islington
Riversdale Road, London N5

The adjudicator could not identify, from the video and the stills produced by the local authority, the sign and as “neither the video film or the stills showed the vehicle contravening any sub-signage” the Appeal was allowed.  There was also no evidence that the weight of the vehicle had exceeded 7.5tons.  The Appeal was allowed.  Once again, the contravention is failing to comply with a sign and it is incumbent on the Local Authority to produce evidence that the signs were clear, correctly installed, facing the right direction and not obscured.

 

CASE NO: 207 0015337 – London Borough of Lambeth
Strathleven Road

The Appeal was allowed because the Appellant stated that the vehicle photographed was not actually his vehicle, ie it had been cloned.  The adjudicator said there was a difference in the style and the format of the number plates and the Appellant’s monologue is missing from the number plate which has been photographed.  The CCTV footage which I have watched a number of times was of very poor quality”.  The Appeal was allowed.  We hear more and more about vehicles being cloned.  Obviously you are not liable to pay a Penalty Charge Notice if it did not relate to your vehicle.

 

CASE NO: 206 0565535 – London Borough of Camden
Lambs Conduit Street, WC1

The vehicle was making a delivery to a property in Lambs Conduit Street although the video evidence showed the vehicle driving along the prohibited route and out the other side.  However, the Traffic Management Order stated that goods could be loaded or unloaded at “any premises in Lambs Conduit Street”.  The Appeal was allowed because although the premises to which goods were being delivered was outside the prohibited section of Lambs Conduit Street, the Traffic Management Order referred to the whole of Lambs Conduit Street. The Appeal was allowed.  It is always worth checking the Traffic Management Order as quite often vehicles are prohibited in only one part of the street but if the Traffic Management Order allows loading in the whole street, then you are entitled to drive through the prohibited section.

 

CASE NO: 207 0151324 – Hammersmith & Fulham
Fulham Road

The Appellant passed the sign which prohibited all motor vehicles.  “But argues that no breach occurred because he dismounted in Fulham Road and pushed the machine only remounting and riding off into Moorpark Road once passed the sign”.  The Appellant stated “the signs referred to only a prohibition of motor vehicles at the actual location of the sign and not before it or after it.  The fact that I did this is not, I believe, an offence”.

The adjudicator stated that “I do not accept that pushing rather than riding a motorcycle passed the sign prohibiting motor vehicles makes any difference to whether a contravention has occurred”.    The Appeal would not have been allowed on this basis.  However, the prohibited area was only a few meters in length and the sign prohibiting motor vehicles were erected at the end of the stretch rather than the beginning so that at the time a motorist would have reached the sign, they would already have committed a contravention so the Appeal was allowed on this basis.  An interesting point which was used on another similar case at this exact location where a motorist dismounted and wheeled his motorcycle across the small prohibited area.  As the motorcycle would still have been caused to pass the signs, whether or not it had been ridden or not, a contravention would have occurred.  Luckily in this case, the Council have positioned the signs at the end of the restricted area rather than the beginning!

 

CASE NO: 207 0152950 – London Borough of Harrow
Camrose Avenue

This Appeal was allowed because “the local authority has failed to provide any evidence of the signage with which it is alleged there was failure to comply.  I have, on previous occasions, allowed Appeals on the basis of that signage of restrictions on Camrose Avenue was not compliant with the Traffic Signs Regulations and General Directions 2002”.  The Appeal was allowed.  It is an absolute disgrace that a local authority would continue to enforce restrictions when they know their signage does not comply.  This is also illegal.

 

CASE NO: 206 0096740 – Camden Council
Laystall Street

This was an interesting one as the Council rejected the Applicant’s formal Appeal but on the Notice of Rejection it referred to the Applicant’s Right of Appeal “28 days from the date hereof” whereas the Act requires the reference to say “28 days from the date of service” – which must, ‘inevitably’ be after 28 days from the date of issue.   This is the second Appeal to be upheld on this basis, the first was by the National Parking Adjudication Service in the case of Lukha v Aylesbury Vale District Council.  The Appeal was allowed.  Not only must the wording on the Penalty Charge Notice comply but also the text on the Notice of Rejection must comply.

 

CASE NO: 207 0107195 – Borough of Islington
Morris Place

This is an interesting case as the Appellant said he did not receive the initial Penalty Charge Notice because it may either have been intercepted or because there were so many flats in his block and the postman delivered to a central point.  The adjudicator said “that the crucial Penalty Charge Notice was not served effectively that is to say ‘actually delivered’”.   The Appeal was allowed.  The local authority has to prove that the Penalty Charge Notice is correctly served.  Simply posting it to a motorist is not proof that the motorist actually received it – an interesting point!

 

CASE NO: 206 0292035 – Transport for London
Battersea Bridge

The position on this is that the owner of the vehicle said that he did not receive the original Penalty Charge Notice.   Transport for London could only show that the PCN was printed.  But, in any event, the adjudicator accepted that the Appellant did not receive the Penalty Charge Notice and therefore the Appeal was allowed.   It is up to the local authority to ensure that a Penalty Charge Notice is received.

 

CASE NO: 207 0117279 – Transport for London
Battersea Bridge Road/ Westbridge Road

This is an interesting case where a vehicle was hired but that the person who received the Penalty Charge Notice said that it was not his signature on the hire agreement.  The adjudicator said that “the position in law is that if the hire agreement is not signed, the Appellant is not deemed to be the owner.  On the evidence as it stands, I am not satisfied that the signature on the hiring agreement is the Appellant’s and I am therefore not satisfied that he is liable as the owner of the vehicle”.  The Appeal was allowed.  If you have not hired a vehicle or signed the hire agreement, then liability for a Penalty Charge Notice cannot be passed to you.

 

CASE NO: 207 000963A – London Borough of Croydon
North End Croydon

The adjudicator was satisfied that the contravention occurred but that the owner had sold his car and produced the letter from the DVLA confirming this.   The Appeal was allowed.  You are not liable for a Penalty Charge Notice in respect of a vehicle that you did not own at the time.

 

206 – 0543532 – London Borough of Islington
Leonard Street

This case was won because the Notice of Rejection issued by the Local Authority was not compliant with Schedule 6, paragraph 4 of the Road Traffic Act 1991 which states as follows:-

“Where any representations are made under paragraph 2 above but the Local Authority concerned do not accept that a ground has been established, the Notice served under paragraph 2(7) above (the Notice of Rejection) must:

  1. a)    state that a Charge Certificate may be served under Paragraph 6 below unless before the end of the period of 28 days beginning with the date of service of the Notice of Rejection:

(i)    the Penalty Charge Notice is paid; or
(ii)    the person on whom the Notice is served appeals to a parking adjudicator against a Penalty Charge;

  1. b)    Indicate the nature of a parking adjudicator’s power to award costs against any person appealing to him; and
  2. c)    Describe in general terms the form and manner in which an Appeal to a parking adjudicator must be made and may contain such other information as the Authority consider appropriate.

However, the Notice of Rejection issued by Islington in this instance stated: Therefore within 28 days of the date of this letter, you should either pay the Penalty Charge or Appeal against our decision and if you do not do either – pay or appeal within the 28 day period, we may issue a Charge Certificate.”

It was argued that the wording does not make it clear that the period commences from the date of service of the letter and not the date of the letter itself and that therefore the Notice of Rejection is not compliant.  The Appellant supported his case by reference to another case at MPAS which was Case No: AY05003B (which was Lukha v Aylesbury District Council).

The adjudicator found in favour of the Appellant and stated “I am satisfied that the above authorities indicate that a strict interpretation is required when considering the mandatory requirements of the statute.  The Notice of Rejection in this case does not comply with those requirements and is therefore invalid and accordingly I must allow the Appeal”.  There are very strict guidelines on not just the wording of a Penalty Charge Notice but also other Notices that may be served during the process such as, in this case, the Notice of Rejection.  It is important to note that the 28 day period section on a Notice of Rejection must state as follows “The enforcing authority may disregard any such representations which are received by them after the period of 28 days beginning with the date on which the Penalty Charge Notice in question was served”.

 

CASE NO: 206 0542755 – City of Westminster
Oxford Street

In this case, the Penalty Charge Notice showed 6th November 2006 and the date of issue of 6th November 2006, however, the audit trail produced by Westminster clearly showed the adjudicator that the Penalty Charge Notice could not have been issued for at least two days after the 6th November.  The adjudicator allowed the Appeal and said “it is not open to the City of Westminster to produce false copies in evidence!”.

 

CASE NO: 206 0164512 – London Borough of Islington
Theberton Street

In this case there was no date of issue on the Notice, only the date of contravention.  Therefore the Appeal was allowed.  A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful even if the 2 dates are the same.

 

CASE NO: 206 0176963 – London Borough of Islington
Theberton Street

As was the case before, the Penalty Charge Notice did not state the date of the Notice “it is therefore invalid and unenforceable and I must accordingly allow this Appeal”. A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful even if the dates are the same.

 

CASE NO: 206 0109514 – City of Westminster
Oxford Street

This is another case where Westminster’s Penalty Charge Notice that did not show the date of the Notice.  The adjudicator said “the PCN issued to the Appellant’s vehicle records the date of the alleged contravention but does not include the “date of the Notice”.  The PCN does not therefore comply with Section 66.  It is consequently invalid and the Appellant is not obliged to pay the Penalty Charge”.  The Appeal was allowed.  A Penalty Charge Notice must state both the date of issue and the date that the contravention actually occurred for it to be lawful.

 

CASE NO: 207 0143971 – Waltham Forest Council
Hoe Street E17

This case concerned a Penalty Charge Notice that was dated over 28 days after the alleged contravention.  Also, no unit of currency was specified for the full penalty charge.  The adjudicator said that “either A or B would be fatal”.  Therefore, the adjudicator did not need to adjudicate.

Quite unbelievable that an authority would try and obtain money in this way.
